sound_type: chi-CA-go assembly call -- iconic three-syllable call with stressed second syllable, loud and carrying, used to reassemble scattered covey; E=4
ecosystem: Brushy open -- chaparral, brushy edges, parks, gardens, agricultural borders, open woodland with understory. Interface between cover and open ground.
season: Year-round resident. Assembly call year-round but most frequent spring (breeding) and autumn (covey reformation).
family: Odontophoridae (FIRST entry. New World quail family enters the engine.)

coordinates.description: California Quail in brushy edges and open woodland of western Washington. Common in suburban and rural habitat with appropriate brush cover.

taxonomy_note.description: FIRST Odontophoridae entry. New World quail family. Distinct from Old World Phasianidae. Smaller body size. Social covey behavior. Forward-curving topknot plume. Western Hemisphere endemic. Chi-CA-go assembly call.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Odontophoridae: first entry. California Quail (159). Mountain Quail (160) will follow.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Callipepla from Greek 'kallos' (beauty) + 'peplos' (robe/garment) -- beautifully robed. californica -- of California.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Callipepla -- first entry. New World quail genus with distinctive head plumes.
taxonomy_note.family_position: First Odontophoridae. New family. Chi-CA-go assembly call. Covey behavior. Topknot plume.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Chi-CA-go: 1500-4000 Hz. Clear, penetrating, within human hearing range.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 2000-3500 Hz. The stressed second syllable (CA) carries the most energy.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Single call: 0.5-0.8 seconds. Repeated dozens of times over several minutes.
song_metrics.note_structure: CHI-CA-GO: Three syllables. First (chi): quick, quiet. Second (CA): loud, stressed, peak. Third (go): dropping pitch and volume. Resembles 'Chicago' pronunciation.
song_metrics.note_types: Chi-CA-go assembly call (primary -- vocal), contact clucks 'pit-pit-pit' (covey cohesion -- vocal), alarm 'pip/kwip' (predator alert -- vocal).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Persistent repetition of chi-CA-go from elevated perch. Dozens of calls in succession. Transitions to softer contact clucks as covey reassembles.
song_metrics.timbre: Clear, emphatic, carrying. The stressed second syllable has a ringing quality. Penetrates brushy habitat.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Loud for body size. 100-300 meter carry. Designed to penetrate dense brush cover.
song_metrics.tempo: Regular repetition. One call every 2-4 seconds when actively assembling.
song_metrics.context: Covey separation. Breeding territory. General assembly. Year-round but peak in spring and autumn.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. The chi-CA-go pattern is highly conserved across the range.
song_metrics.key_property: CHI-CA-GO ASSEMBLY CALL -- three syllables, stressed second, 100-300m carry. FIRST ODONTOPHORIDAE. New World quail. Covey behavior. Topknot plume. E=4. One of the most recognizable PNW bird calls.

plumage.overall: Males: blue-gray breast, scaled belly, chestnut flanks with white streaks, black throat bordered by white stripe, brown back and wings. Females: plainer brown overall, scaled belly. Both: forward-curving topknot plume.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Forward-curving topknot (comma-shaped, 6 feathers). Males: black throat with white border. Scaled belly pattern.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. The topknot is present year-round.
plumage.flight_pattern: Burst flight when flushed. Whirring wings. Short distance. Prefers to run through brush.

physical_description.overall: Length: 24-27 cm. Weight: 170-200 g. Small, plump gamebird. Forward-curving topknot plume on forehead.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Topknot plume. Rounded body. Short tail. Terrestrial foraging posture.
physical_description.field_id: Small plump quail with forward-curving topknot. In coveys of 10-200+. Listen for 'chi-CA-go!' assembly call from elevated perch. Brushy habitat near openings. Runs through brush when disturbed.

nodes.title: Chi-CA-Go Assembly Call
nodes.description: Three syllables, stressed second. 100-300 meter carry. Assembly function: brings scattered covey together from brushy habitat. One of the most recognizable bird calls in the PNW. Persistent repetition from elevated perch.

nodes.title: Covey Social Behavior
nodes.description: Fundamental social unit: 10-200+ birds. Communal roosting (rosette formation). Group foraging. Flush-scatter-reassemble cycle. Contact clucks maintain cohesion. Assembly call reunites after separation.

nodes.title: First Odontophoridae -- New Family
nodes.description: New World quail family enters the engine. Distinct from Old World Phasianidae. Smaller body. Social coveys. Head plumes. Western Hemisphere endemic. Family transition from Phasianidae (153-158) to Odontophoridae (159-160).

nodes.title: Forward-Curving Topknot Plume
nodes.description: Cluster of 6 overlapping feathers curving forward from crown. Comma-shaped. Bobs when walking. Both sexes but male larger. Cultural icon. State bird of California. Visual species recognition signal.

cross_references.relationship: FAMILY TRANSITION! Final Phasianidae (158) to first Odontophoridae (159). Old World to New World. Alpine silence to brushy assembly call.
ODONTOPHORIDAE PAIR! California Quail (159) and Mountain Quail (160). Two New World quail. Forward plume vs. straight plume. Chi-CA-go vs. quee-ark.
GALLIFORMES CONTRAST! Turkey: Phasianidae, largest, E=6. California Quail: Odontophoridae, much smaller, E=4. Same order, different families.
SIZE CONTRAST! Spruce Grouse (450-650g, quiet). California Quail (170-200g, loud chi-CA-go). Smaller bird, louder call. Energy inverted from size.
